output_summary: profiled datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small.
This run produced an initial draft of the pipeline.
The pipeline loads the three book datasets from parquet files and aligns Goodreads and Metabooks to the Amazon schema using an LLM-based schema matcher.
The pipeline adds normalized matching fields title_norm and author_norm for all datasets and publish_year_num when publish_year is present.
The pipeline performs pairwise blocking across all dataset pairs using TokenBlocker on title_norm with word bigrams, minimum token length 3, and id as the record key.

The pipeline performs pairwise rule-based entity matching with comparators on title_norm, author_norm, publish_year_num, and publisher, using weights of 0.5, 0.3, 0.1, and 0.1 and a threshold of 0.75.
The pipeline concatenates all pairwise correspondences and applies a new fusion strategy with longest_string for title, author, and publisher and maximum for publish_year.
Fusion is centered on rule-based correspondences generated from normalized title, author, year, and publisher similarity rather than any trust-based or voting-based fusion behavior.
Matching behavior emphasizes title as the strongest identity signal through both token blocking on title_norm and the highest comparator weight on title_norm.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an initial draft of the evaluation script for the current fused output.

The dominant strategy was to build an evaluation-aligned fused view by mapping fused clusters to gold IDs and then apply per-field matching functions within a DataFusionStrategy evaluated by DataFusionEvaluator.

The evaluation used tokenized matching for title, author, publisher, and language, year-only matching for publish_year, set-equality matching for detected list-like fields and genres, and numeric-tolerance matching for page_count when present in both datasets.
Textual bibliographic fields were grouped under tokenized matching, specifically title, author, publisher, and conditionally language.
Temporal fields were grouped under year-only matching through publish_year.
List-like and set-valued fields were grouped under set-equality matching via detected list-like columns and the explicit genres field.
Numeric magnitude fields were grouped under numeric-tolerance matching through the explicit page_count field when available in both fused and gold data.
The script added helper functions to parse _fusion_sources from JSON or Python-literal list formats and return normalized source ID lists.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 59.29%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
This run was a refinement of the existing evaluation pipeline.

The matching stage kept token blocking on normalized titles but strengthened rule-based matching by changing the comparator weights from [0.5, 0.3, 0.1, 0.1] to [0.6, 0.25, 0.1, 0.05] and raising the threshold from 0.75 to 0.82.
The publisher comparator was switched from raw publisher text with lowercase preprocessing to a normalized publisher_norm field.
Fusion replaced generic longest_string and maximum rules with custom fusers for title, author, publisher, and publish_year.
Fusion added explicit fusers for language, page_count, and genres, with genres fused via union.
The pipeline shifted toward higher-precision entity matching by emphasizing title similarity more heavily and accepting fewer matches through the higher threshold.

Fusion shifted from generic value selection toward attribute-specific decision rules that prefer cleaner authors, consensus years, modal languages, larger consensus page counts, and merged genre sets.

The run added html and regex based normalization helpers for title, author, publisher, language, and genres, and created normalized fields including title_norm, author_norm, publisher_norm, publish_year_num, and page_count_num.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was a refinement of the evaluation script in evaluation_refinement mode.

The dominant strategy was to make evaluation functions explicit per book field, using tokenized matching for title, author, publisher, and language, year-only matching for publish_year, numeric tolerance for page_count, and set equality for genres and other detected list-like columns.
Textual scalar fields were grouped under tokenized matching, specifically title, author, publisher, and language.
Temporal fields were handled separately with year_only_match for publish_year.
Numeric fields were handled separately with numeric_tolerance_match for page_count.
Collection or list-like fields were grouped under set_equality_match, including genres and any additional detected list-like columns that existed in both fused and gold data.

The ID alignment logic was refined to prefer source identifiers with the most common gold ID prefix, then fall back to exact gold ID matches, and finally fall back to the cluster _id when no source match was found.
The helper for gold-prefix detection was simplified from returning multiple prefixes to returning the single most common prefix or None when no prefix existed.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 53.57%; previous logged accuracy was 59.29%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement of the existing pipeline.
The blocking strategy changed from TokenBlocker on title_norm to SortedNeighbourhoodBlocker keyed on title_norm with a window of 15 for all dataset pairs.
The matcher was tightened by changing weights from [0.6, 0.25, 0.1, 0.05] to [0.58, 0.27, 0.1, 0.05] and raising the match threshold from 0.82 to 0.86 across all pairwise matches.
The genres fusion rule changed from union to a custom choose_genres fuser.
Normalization and fusion logic were revised for several fields, including title, publisher, language, page_count, publish_year, author, and genres.

The run shifted matching behavior toward higher-precision candidate generation and acceptance by replacing token blocking with sorted-neighbourhood blocking and using a stricter correspondence threshold.
The run shifted fusion behavior for genres away from taking the full union of source values and toward selecting repeated genres first, with a fallback to the top three most frequent values.
The run also strengthened canonicalization before matching by removing title articles during title normalization and standardizing language aliases such as english, eng, and en to English.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.

The dominant strategy kept field-specific evaluators aligned to the book schema by using tokenized matching for title, author, language, and publisher, year-only matching for publish_year, numeric tolerance matching for page_count, and set equality for genres and detected list-like columns.
The run retained the eval_id mapping view built from _fusion_sources and _id so evaluation compared fused rows against the gold records through mapped identifiers rather than raw cluster ids alone.
Textual scalar fields were grouped under tokenized_match, specifically title, author, language, and publisher.
Temporal and numeric fields were grouped under specialized comparators, with publish_year using year_only_match and page_count using numeric_tolerance_match.
Collection-style fields were grouped under set_equality_match, including genres and any detected list-like columns.
The code kept helper functions for parsing _fusion_sources, detecting the dominant gold id prefix, and expanding fused rows into an evaluation view keyed by eval_id.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 53.57%; evaluation attempt counter is 3.
